# TRIZ / ARIZ Working Template

A practical, fill-in-the-blanks problem-solving template distilled from
ARIZ-85C (MATRIZ), the 1998 "Americanized ARIZ" framework, and the
classical Contradiction Matrix. Reorganized for **conceptual and engineering
problems**, not just mechanical patents.

> **How to use this file.** Copy it per problem. Work top to bottom.
> Stop early the moment a solution concept becomes obvious — ARIZ is
> ~50% reformulation, and most problems dissolve before you reach the
> matrix. The matrix and principles (Part B) are a *lookup* you jump to
> from Part A, not a linear stage.

---

## Part 0 — Frame the problem (5 min, do not skip)

| Field | Your entry |
|---|---|
| **Key problem** (one sentence, no solution words) | |
| **System** (the part/space where the problem occurs) | |
| **Main function** of that system (verb + object: "to *hold* the substrate") | |
| **Why now / cost of not solving** | |
| **Constraint I am tempted to treat as fixed** (name it — it is usually the lever) | |

**Solution-neutrality check:** rewrite the key problem so it contains *no
implied solution*. "Make the bracket stiffer" → "Prevent deflection under
load." If you can't, you're still describing a solution, not a problem.

---

# PART A — REFORMULATE (the ARIZ spine)

## A1. Components & functions

- **Components / subsystems:** _(list; long is fine)_
- **Supersystem / environment:** _
- **Useful functions:** _
- **Harmful / insufficient / excessive functions:** _

Simple labelled sketch (or describe): _

## A2. State the two technical contradictions

A *technical contradiction* = improving one thing worsens another.
Write it **both ways**; you don't yet know which serves the main function best.

| | IF (action/change) | THEN (improves) | BUT (worsens) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TC-1** | | | |
| **TC-2** (inverse of TC-1) | | | |

## A3. Intensify (push to the extreme — this is where quality comes from)

| | Extreme statement |
|---|---|
| **TC-1 intensified** | harmful action *fully* eliminated, but useful action *not performed at all* |
| **TC-2 intensified** | useful action *fully* performed, but harmful action is *as bad as possible* |

> Tip from ARIZ-85C: "few elements" → push to **zero elements**;
> "weak/strong" → push to **absent/infinite**. Extremes break inertia.

**Select** the intensified version that best preserves the **main function** → _
*(Keep both; you may revisit the rejected one in A8.)*

## A4. Conflicting pair (max 3 elements)

| Role | Element | State 1 | State 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Product** (object acted on) | | | |
| **Tool** (object directly conflicting with product) | | | |

## A5. Resources (the raw material of "free" solutions)

The ideal solution uses what's already present. Inventory aggressively —
**introducing new substances/fields is discouraged.**

| Resource type | Internal (tool / product) | External (environment / supersystem) |
|---|---|---|
| **Substances** | | |
| **Fields** (mech, thermal, EM, chemical, gravitational…) | | |
| **Space** | | |
| **Time** | | |
| **Derived / modified** (combinations, phase changes, by-products, voids) | | |

## A6. Operating Zone & Operating Time

| | Where / when the conflict lives |
|---|---|
| **OZ-1** (useful action) | |
| **OZ-2** (harmful action) | |
| OZ overlap? | yes / no — sketch |
| **OT-1 / OT-2** (before · during · after) | |
| OT overlap? | yes / no |

*(If the conflict splits cleanly in space or time, you may already have a
separation-principle solution — jump to B3.)*

## A7. Ideal Final Result & Physical Contradiction

**IFR (X-element form):**
> The **X-element**, using only existing resources, preserves the ability to
> _(main useful function)_ inside _(OZ)_ during _(OT)_, and eliminates
> _(harmful effect)_ — **without** added complexity or new harm.

Now substitute real resources for "X-element" (5–6 best candidates from A5):

| Candidate resource | IFR statement holds? Notes |
|---|---|
| | |
| | |

**Physical contradiction** (the same parameter must take opposite values):

| | Macro level | Micro level (particle / molecular) |
|---|---|---|
| The **parameter** _ of _ | should be **___** to provide useful function | |
| **BUT** | should be **___** to eliminate harm | |

> The micro restatement ("what must each particle do?") often reveals
> interactions hidden at component scale, and strips away inertia-laden
> vocabulary. Use **Smart Little People**: imagine the operating zone filled
> with tiny agents free to act however needed — what would they do to satisfy
> both opposing states at once?

## A8. If still stuck — change the problem, not the effort

- [ ] Is this actually 2+ problems? Split them.
- [ ] Switch to the **other** technical contradiction (the one you parked in A3).
- [ ] Reformulate the mini-problem with a different main function.
- [ ] Restate at the **supersystem** level.
- [ ] "One step back from IFR": deliberately degrade/disassemble the ideal —
      the easier nearby problem often hands you the real solution.

---

# PART B — SOLVE (knowledge-base lookups)

Reach here from A7/A8. Use in this order: **Separation → 76 Standards →
Contradiction Matrix → 40 Principles → Effects.**

## B3. Separation principles (for the *physical* contradiction)

When one parameter must be X and not-X, separate the requirement by:

| Separate by… | Ask | Typical principles |
|---|---|---|
| **Space** | Can X hold *here* and not-X *there*? | 1,2,3,4,7,13,17,24,26,30 |
| **Time** | Can X hold *now* and not-X *later*? | 9,10,11,15,16,18,19,20,21,29,34,37 |
| **Scale / condition** (system vs part) | X at whole-system level, not-X at component level (or by phase/state)? | 1,3,5,12,28,31,32,35,36,38,40 |
| **On condition** | X under one condition, not-X under another | 28,31,32,35,36 |

## B4. Contradiction Matrix — reformulated as a lookup

The classic 39×39 grid is unusable by hand. Use this **two-step lookup**
instead. (1) Map your "improving" and "worsening" terms to the nearest of the
39 parameters below. (2) Read the recommended principles from the abridged
high-yield matrix.

### The 39 parameters (grouped for fast scanning)

**Physical / geometric**
1 Weight (moving) · 2 Weight (stationary) · 3 Length (moving) · 4 Length
(stationary) · 5 Area (moving) · 6 Area (stationary) · 7 Volume (moving) ·
8 Volume (stationary) · 12 Shape · 17 Temperature · 18 Illumination/brightness

**Dynamics / mechanics**
9 Speed · 10 Force · 11 Stress/pressure · 13 Stability of object ·
14 Strength · 15 Duration of action (moving) · 16 Duration of action
(stationary) · 19 Energy used (moving) · 20 Energy used (stationary) ·
21 Power · 22 Loss of energy · 23 Loss of substance · 24 Loss of information ·
25 Loss of time · 26 Quantity of substance

**Quality / performance**
27 Reliability · 28 Measurement accuracy · 29 Manufacturing precision ·
30 Harmful factors acting *on* object · 31 Harmful factors *generated* by
object

**System-level**
32 Ease of manufacture · 33 Ease of operation · 34 Ease of repair ·
35 Adaptability/versatility · 36 Device complexity · 37 Difficulty of
detecting/measuring · 38 Extent of automation · 39 Productivity

> **Conceptual-domain translation** (for non-mechanical problems — software,
> research, organizations): read parameters as analogies.
> *Weight/volume → resource footprint or code size.*
> *Speed → throughput/latency.* *Strength → robustness.*
> *Stability → invariance under change.* *Complexity → coupling/cognitive load.*
> *Loss of information → observability.* *Adaptability → generality.*
> *Harmful factors → side effects / technical debt.*

### High-yield abridged matrix (most common engineering conflicts)

Improving ↓ / Worsening → : principles to try (by number, see B5).

| Improving \ Worsening | Weight/size | Speed | Strength | Reliability | Energy/loss | Complexity | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Weight / size** | — | 13,28,15,19 | 1,8,40,15 | 1,8,15,34 | 35,12,34,31 | 26,30,36,34 | 28,32,1,24 |
| **Speed** | 2,28,13,38 | — | 13,14,8 | 11,35,27,28 | 19,35,38,2 | 10,28,32,1 | 28,32,1,24 |
| **Strength** | 40,26,27,1 | 8,13,26,14 | — | 11,3,10,32 | 19,35,10 | 2,13,28 | 3,27,16 |
| **Reliability** | 3,8,10,40 | 21,35,11,28 | 11,28 | — | 21,11,27,19 | 13,35,1 | 32,3,11,23 |
| **Energy / loss** | 6,2,34,19 | 16,35,38 | 35,10,14 | 11,10,35 | — | 7,23,35,2 | 35,18,10 |
| **Complexity** | 26,30,34,36 | 10,28 | 1,26,13 | 13,35,1 | 2,22,17,19 | — | 27,26,28 |
| **Accuracy** | 26,28,32,3 | 28,32,1 | 3,27,40 | 32,3,11 | 35,18,10 | 26,24,32 | — |

*This is a working subset; for any pair outside it, consult the full matrix
(matriz.org or any TRIZ matrix tool), but the principles above resolve a large
fraction of real conflicts. Empty/—  = no strong pattern; go to separation
principles or Effects.*

## B5. The 40 Inventive Principles (reference)

1. **Segmentation** — divide into independent parts; make sectional/modular.
2. **Taking out / extraction** — separate the interfering part or property.
3. **Local quality** — make parts non-uniform; each part does its own job.
4. **Asymmetry** — replace symmetric form with asymmetric.
5. **Merging / consolidation** — combine identical or parallel operations.
6. **Universality** — one part performs several functions; eliminate others.
7. **Nested doll (matryoshka)** — place objects inside one another.
8. **Anti-weight / counterweight** — compensate weight with lift/buoyancy.
9. **Preliminary anti-action** — pre-stress in the opposite direction.
10. **Preliminary action** — do it (partly) in advance; pre-arrange.
11. **Beforehand cushioning** — prepare emergency means in advance.
12. **Equipotentiality** — remove the need to raise/lower in a field.
13. **The other way around** — invert the action; make movable fixed / vice versa.
14. **Spheroidality / curvature** — use curves, rollers, spirals instead of lines.
15. **Dynamics** — make rigid things adjustable/movable; optimize per stage.
16. **Partial or excessive action** — if 100% is hard, do slightly less/more.
17. **Another dimension** — move to 2D/3D; tilt; use the other side; stack.
18. **Mechanical vibration** — oscillate; use resonance; ultrasound.
19. **Periodic action** — replace continuous with pulsed; use pauses.
20. **Continuity of useful action** — eliminate idle/intermittent strokes.
21. **Skipping / rushing through** — run a harmful stage at high speed.
22. **Blessing in disguise** — turn a harm into a benefit.
23. **Feedback** — introduce or change feedback; make it adaptive.
24. **Intermediary** — use a temporary carrier or go-between, then remove it.
25. **Self-service** — system serves/repairs itself; use waste.
26. **Copying** — use cheap copies/optical/virtual instead of the real thing.
27. **Cheap short-living objects** — replace expensive durable with cheap disposable.
28. **Mechanics substitution** — replace mechanical with field (EM, acoustic, optical).
29. **Pneumatics / hydraulics** — use gas/liquid instead of solid parts.
30. **Flexible shells / thin films** — use membranes, films, isolation layers.
31. **Porous materials** — make porous; fill pores with a useful substance.
32. **Color / optical changes** — change color, transparency, visibility.
33. **Homogeneity** — interacting objects from the same (or matched) material.
34. **Discarding & recovering** — let parts disappear/dissolve after use; restore consumables.
35. **Parameter change** — change phase, concentration, temperature, flexibility.
36. **Phase transitions** — exploit volume/heat changes during phase change.
37. **Thermal expansion** — use expansion/contraction; bimetals.
38. **Strong oxidants / enriched atmosphere** — use enriched/active media.
39. **Inert atmosphere** — replace normal environment with inert/vacuum.
40. **Composite materials** — replace uniform with composite/multi-material.

## B6. Standard Solutions & Effects (when principles don't land)

- **Su-Field check:** model the conflict as Substance1 — Field — Substance2.
  Incomplete field? → add the missing one. Harmful field? → block, counter, or
  convert it. (This is the gateway to the 76 Standard Solutions.)
- **Effects database:** if you need a *function* ("move liquid without a pump",
  "measure temperature without contact"), look it up in a scientific-effects
  index rather than inventing — there is almost always a known physical effect.

---

# PART C — VERIFY & HARVEST

## C1. Did it actually work?

- [ ] Was the **physical contradiction removed** (not compromised)?
- [ ] Did the solution use **only resources from A5** (no smuggled new substances/fields)?
- [ ] Does it satisfy the **IFR** / main function?
- [ ] Is it **implementable** in the real system?
- [ ] Any **new** harmful effects introduced? List them → these are your next problem.

## C2. Maximize the win

- What must change in the **supersystem** to deploy this?
- Does the changed system enable **new applications**?
- **Generalize** the solution into a *method*. State it, then state its
  *inverse*, and test both against your other open problems.
- How does it scale as size/time/cost → 0 or → ∞?

## C3. Capture the learning

- Where did your actual path diverge from this template? (That delta is the lesson.)
- What makes this solution distinct from the standard effect/principle it came from?
- Add to your own solutions database.

---

### One-page flow (memory aid)

```
Frame → solution-neutral problem statement
  │
  ▼  PART A: REFORMULATE
Components/functions → Two TCs → Intensify → Conflicting pair
  → Resources → OZ/OT → IFR + Physical Contradiction
  → (stuck? change the problem)
  │
  ▼  PART B: SOLVE
Separate (space/time/scale/condition)
  → 76 Standards (Su-Field) → Matrix lookup → 40 Principles → Effects
  │
  ▼  PART C: VERIFY & HARVEST
Contradiction removed? resources-only? IFR met?
  → generalize → capture
```

*Sources distilled: ARIZ-85C (MATRIZ); Marconi, "ARIZ: An Americanized
Learning Framework," TRIZ Journal 1998; Altshuller's 39 parameters / 40
principles / separation principles.*
